What is the relationship between OHM and TOPO instances?

title: TOPO CLI — industrial-specific commands on OHM architecture
description: Implement topo CLI as a specialized instantiation of the OHM architecture. Commands: topo graph impact <equipment-id> (failure propagation), topo graph topology <area-id> (physical connectivity), topo graph causal-chain <effect> (root cause analysis), topo observe <sensor-id> --value <v> (ingest telemetry), topo alarm <equipment-id> --type <type> (create alarm node). Both ohmd and topod use the same binary with different configs. **Design decisions needed:** code organization (shared library vs config-driven single binary), daemon naming convention, config file discovery for each instance. **PM input needed:** should ohmd and topod run on the same host or separate hosts? title: Confidence aggregation — how to combine multiple agent observations
description:
VISION.md says 'accumulate, don't collapse' — three observations on the same node stay as three separate rows. Downstream consumers choose how to aggregate.

But the graph doesn't help them. When I query a node's observations, I get three rows with different values and confidences. There's no guidance on how to combine them.

Design question: Should OHM provide aggregation functions, or leave it entirely to the consumer?

Options:
1. Pure accumulate — store only, consumer aggregates (current design)
2. Provide aggregation hints — add aggregation_strategy to node metadata ('mean', 'max', 'bayesian', 'weighted')
3. Compute derived confidence — when 3 agents observe the same thing, compute a combined confidence score using Bayesian updating or inverse-variance weighting, store it as a separate L2 edge

Option 1 is cleanest but pushes complexity to every consumer. Option 3 is most useful but requires defining the aggregation function.

For now: keep Option 1 (pure accumulate). But add a note in the schema docs that this is an open design question, and add observation count + mean/min/max confidence to the stats endpoint so consumers can make informed decisions without querying every observation.

Acceptance:
- stats endpoint includes observation_count and confidence distribution per node
- ADR or docs note that aggregation strategy is an open design question
- No changes to the accumulation model (keep current behavior)
close_reason: Implemented in 5cd151c. Observation stats (count, mean, min, max confidence) added to /stats endpoint.

title: Substrate services — memory, connections, attention, decay, provenance, deduplication
description:

10 services:

4. Decay and staleness (P1) — confidence auto-decays, 30-day half-life
5. Contradiction detection (P1) — flag conflicting observations/challenges
6. Connection discovery (P1) — suggest links, find orphans, detect clusters
7. Context retrieval (P2) — relevance scoring, graph-proximity-based
8. Deduplication (P2) — link independent observations of same event
9. Confidence calibration (P2) — track agent over/underconfidence by domain
10. Synthesis prompts (P2) — prompt agents when clusters reach critical mass

Line: substrate does pattern matching and bookkeeping. Agents do meaning-making.

title: Change feed consumer — push notifications for agents
description:
ohm graph listen exists but agents can only poll for changes. For a 'shared awareness' system, push notifications matter — agents should be able to subscribe to changes in their domain.

Current: agent calls listen() periodically, gets changes since last call.
Needed: agent subscribes to change types/layers, gets notified when relevant changes happen.

Options:
1. WebSocket in ohmd — agents connect, subscribe to layers/edge_types, get push updates
2. Webhook — agent registers a URL, ohmd POSTs changes
3. Polling with long-poll — listen?since=<last>&timeout=30 blocks until new changes
4. Event queue — each agent has a queue, changes are pushed to relevant queues

Option 3 (long-poll) is simplest and doesn't require WebSocket infrastructure. Option 1 is best for real-time.

Acceptance:
- Agents can subscribe to specific layers and edge types
- Changes are delivered within seconds, not minutes
- Backpressure: if agent falls behind, changes queue up to a limit then oldest are dropped
- Change feed includes: who made the change, what changed, old and new values

title: SDK missing basic read methods: get_node(), get_edge(), find_or_create(), search()

The SDK (sdk.py) has no read methods for single entities. Agents using the SDK can:

title: sdk.py update_edge() uses dynamic SQL with f-string (potential SQL injection risk)
description: sdk.py update_edge() constructs SQL with an f-string: f'UPDATE ohm_edges SET {chr(39)}, {chr(39)}.join(updates)} WHERE id = ?'. However, the column names in updates are hardcoded strings set by the code itself ('confidence = ?', 'provenance = ?', 'condition = ?', 'updated_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP', 'updated_by = ?') — they are NOT user input. The actual values are parameterized with ?. This is NOT a SQL injection vulnerability. The f-string is a code style concern (dynamic SQL construction pattern that could be copied unsafely) but not a security bug. RECOMMENDATION: Close as not-a-bug, or refactor to use explicit column mapping for clarity. If refactoring, consider using a dict-based approach: updates = {'confidence': confidence, 'provenance': provenance, ...} and then build the SET clause from dict keys.

title: Critical: Server routes use ohmd as agent_name — boundary enforcement broken in production
description:
## Bug: All server writes attributed to 'ohmd' instead of authenticated agent

### Problem
The OHM daemon creates a single OhmStore with agent_name='ohmd' at startup. All HTTP handlers that call self.store.write_node(), self.store.write_edge(), self.store.write_observation(), self.store.update_edge_confidence(), and self.store.update_agent_state() use this single store — meaning ALL edges are created_by='ohmd' regardless of which agent authenticated.

### Impact
- Boundary enforcement is completely disabled in production. Since every edge belongs to 'ohmd', any agent can 'update' any edge.
- Challenge/support semantics are broken. challenge_edge creates new edges also attributed to 'ohmd'.
- Provenance is lost. You can't tell which agent created what.
- Agent state is broken. update_agent_state() writes to agent_name='ohmd' instead of the authenticated agent.

### Affected Endpoints
- POST /node, /edge, /challenge/{id}, /support/{id}, /observe/{id}, /state — all use self.store with agent_name='ohmd'

### Not Affected
- POST /register — correctly passes 'agent' to create_node/create_edge directly

### Fix (recommended: Option A)
Add agent_name parameter to OhmStore write methods:
  - write_node(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - write_edge(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - write_observation(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - update_edge_confidence(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - challenge_edge(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - update_agent_state(agent_name=agent, ...)
Then update server.py to pass the authenticated agent to each write method.

Option B (per-request wrapper) risks DuckDB connection issues.
Option C (set self.store.agent_name per request) is NOT thread-safe.

### Reproduction
```python
from ohm.store import OhmStore
s = OhmStore(':memory:', 'ohmd')
s.write_node(id='n1', label='test', type='note')
print(s.get_node('n1')['created_by'])  # 'ohmd' — should be the authenticated agent
```

The ohm SDK should support passing tokens via environment variable (OHM_TOKEN) or constructor argument.

- GET endpoints are open (no auth required)

title: Bug: POST /node upserts then raises ConflictError — data modified despite error response
description:
## Bug: POST /node upserts then raises ConflictError on existing node

### Problem
When POST /node receives an ID that already exists, the handler flow is:
1. `self.store.write_node()` is called — it performs an UPDATE (upsert behavior)
2. The store returns `{"created": False, ...}`
3. The server checks `result.get("created", True)` and raises `ConflictError`

The database modification has already happened before the error is raised. The client receives a 409 Conflict error, but the node WAS successfully updated in the database.

### Impact
- Clients get error responses for operations that succeeded
- No way to update an existing node via POST /node (even though store supports it)
- Inconsistent state: database changed, client told it failed

### Fix
Either:
A. Make POST /node truly idempotent — if node exists, return 200 with updated data (remove the ConflictError)
B. Change store.write_node() to not update existing nodes — INSERT only, raise on conflict
C. Add a separate PUT /node/{id} endpoint for updates, keep POST /node for creation only

Option A is recommended for a multi-agent system — agents should be able to re-register/update their nodes.

### Reproduction
```python
from ohm.store import OhmStore
s = OhmStore(':memory:', 'test')
s.write_node(id='n1', label='concept', type='note', content='original')
# Second call with same ID updates the node:
result = s.write_node(id='n1', label='concept', type='note', content='updated')
print(result['created'])  # False
print(result['content'])   # 'updated' — the node WAS updated
# But server.py raises ConflictError here
```

title: Quack protocol integration for concurrent access
description:
Integrate Quack (DuckDB HTTP protocol) into ohmd for concurrent multi-agent access. Currently ohmd uses a simple HTTP server with in-process DuckDB — this is single-writer and blocks on concurrent access. Quack provides proper concurrent read/write with transaction isolation via the DuckDB HTTP protocol.

Approach: Replace the manual HTTP handlers in server.py with Quack for all graph query endpoints. Keep custom endpoints (auth, state, challenge semantics) as thin wrappers that call into Quack. The key insight is that Quack handles concurrent access; ohmd handles OHM-specific logic (boundary enforcement, challenge semantics, agent attribution).

Acceptance:
- ohmd starts with Quack backend for DuckDB
- Multiple agents can read/write concurrently without corruption
- Write operations still enforce boundary rules (challenge semantics, ownership)
- All existing CLI commands work through the Quack-backed daemon
- Benchmark: 10 concurrent agents writing, no data loss or deadlock
- 144 existing tests still pass
notes:
FOUND DURING REVIEW (2026-05-16):
- server.py currently uses http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler — single-threaded, synchronous
- All graph operations go through OhmStore which wraps DuckDB directly — single writer
- Quack provides HTTP protocol for DuckDB, handles concurrent read/write
- Key design: Quack replaces the raw DuckDB connection in server.py, not OhmStore itself
- OhmStore should stay as a convenience wrapper but connect through Quack
- SDK and CLI currently bypass server.py entirely — they connect to DuckDB files directly
- After Quack integration, SDK/CLI should still support direct file access (offline mode)
- Need to decide: does Quack run as a separate process or embedded in ohmd?

title: Parameterized queries — replace f-string interpolation in SQL
description:
From security audit (OHM-5of). validation.py sanitizes identifiers before f-string interpolation, but this is defense-in-depth, not a replacement for parameterized queries.

Five locations use f-strings with validated-but-interpolated values:
1. layer in WHERE clauses (queries/__init__.py lines 53, 119)
2. since (timestamp) in change feed (queries/__init__.py line 312)
3. agent_name in agent state (queries/__init__.py lines 315, 354)
4. Dynamic WHERE in CLI filter (cli/__init__.py line 417)
5. Dynamic UPDATE in SDK (sdk.py line 144)

Principle: Use parameterized queries (? placeholders) wherever possible. Keep validation as defense-in-depth. f-string interpolation should only be for SQL keywords/structure that CANNOT be parameterized (like column names in dynamic ORDER BY).

Acceptance:
- All user-provided values use parameterized queries (?)
- f-string interpolation only for SQL structure that DuckDB can't parameterize
- validation.py remains as defense-in-depth (not removed)
- 134+ tests still pass
- No SQL injection possible even if validation is bypassed
close_reason: Implemented in 6e1ba86. All queries use ? parameterized placeholders. Only remaining f-strings are validation error messages, not SQL.

title: Auth: add --no-auth flag, fail closed by default for production
description:
From security audit (OHM-5of). Currently when self.tokens is empty (default), all requests get agent='ohm' with full access. This is fail-open.

POST routes now use _require_auth() which raises AuthenticationError — good.
GET routes still default to agent='ohm' when no tokens configured — acceptable for dev but not production.

Fix: Add --no-auth CLI flag and OHM_NO_AUTH env var. When set, allow unauthenticated access. When NOT set (default), deny requests without valid tokens.

Behavior:
- --no-auth or OHM_NO_AUTH=1: all requests allowed, agent defaults to 'ohm' (dev mode)
- Default (no flag): POST requires valid token, GET requires valid token OR no tokens configured (transition mode)
- Production: tokens configured, all requests require auth

Acceptance:
- ohmd --no-auth starts in dev mode (no auth required)
- Without --no-auth and with tokens configured, all requests require valid Bearer token
- Without --no-auth and without tokens, GET requests allowed, POST requests denied
- Tests verify fail-closed behavior
close_reason: Implemented in 6e1ba86. --no-auth flag added, fail-closed by default. OHM_NO_AUTH env var also supported. GET routes require auth when tokens configured.

title: Health endpoints /health and /ready return 401 to unauthenticated requests
Path traversal risk in /node/{id} and /edge/{id} endpoints
description: server.py extracts node_id and edge_id directly from URL paths (e.g., path[6:] for /node/{id}) without validation or sanitization. A request like /node/../../etc/passwd or /node/%00 could potentially cause issues. DuckDB parameterized queries protect against SQL injection, but the unsanitized IDs are passed to get_node()/get_edge() lookups. Need to: (1) validate that IDs match the expected format (alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores), (2) reject paths with directory traversal characters, (3) add URL decoding. The validation module already has validate_identifier() — use it in the server handlers.
